steps.title: Identify the clause structures
steps.content:
The sentence has two related units: “It began to dim over the next few weeks” (an independent clause stating an observation) and “although they are extremely powerful, supernovas are short-lived phenomena” (a dependent clause introducing a contrast, followed by an independent clause explaining supernovas).

The word “however” appears in the blank to signal the transition between these ideas — contrasting the observation that this particular supernova dimmed over weeks with the general fact that supernovas are short-lived.

steps.title: Match the correct transition punctuation
steps.content:
When “however” introduces a contrasting thought that includes a dependent clause elaboration, the correct pattern is: independent clause + comma + however + semicolon + dependent clause elaboration.

Here, the comma separates the first clause from the transition word, and the semicolon marks the strong punctuation break before the dependent clause elaboration that follows.

Option B breaks the sentence unnecessarily and creates two thoughts when they should be tightly linked.

Option C uses commas around “however,” as if it interrupts the thought, but “however” is a true transition, not an interrupting parenthetical.

Option D uses a semicolon before “however,” which would require a pure independent clause to follow, but “although” introduces a dependent clause.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
